does: This paper introduces SCDBench, a comprehensive benchmark dataset and evaluation methodology for assessing LLM-based smart contract decompilers. The dataset comprises 600 real-world Solidity contracts paired with bytecode, ground-truth source code, and semantic checkpoints. The evaluation framework uses four cumulative stages progressing from format completeness through compilability, ABI recovery, and semantic consistency verification via differential replay testing. The authors evaluate three major LLMs—Claude Opus, GPT-5.3-Codex, and GLM-5—in zero-shot decompilation settings, including variants with extended reasoning and compilation-repair mechanisms, establishing baseline performance metrics and identifying failure modes where decompilers produce plausible but semantically incorrect code.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in evaluating smart contract decompilers, building on prior research in program synthesis, reverse engineering, and LLM-based code generation. Earlier decompilation studies relied on narrow datasets with inconsistent metrics, lacking robust semantic validation. The paper extends existing work on LLM evaluation methodologies and compilation-based testing frameworks, drawing inspiration from differential testing approaches in compiler verification and combining them with semantic consistency checks through bytecode replay. It responds to the emerging challenge of evaluating LLMs generating source-like code that may appear correct syntactically while violating contract semantics, connecting to broader literature on hallucination detection and code fidelity in neural code generation.
enables: SCDBench establishes a standardized evaluation framework that can drive improvements in LLM-based decompilation techniques and enable systematic comparison of future models. The dataset and methodology support research into semantic-preserving code generation, developing better prompting strategies for bytecode-to-source translation, and improving compilation-aware repair mechanisms. The four-stage evaluation pipeline with semantic checkpoints provides infrastructure for studying where decompilers fail systematically, potentially enabling targeted improvements in contract analysis tools. This work positions smart contract decompilation as a high-stakes code generation domain requiring stronger semantic guarantees, pointing toward verification-aware approaches and hybrid methods combining neural generation with formal consistency checking for blockchain security applications.
